behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both apply: this failure is commonly in real recursive watch registration and OS event delivery for nested paths. An in-process fake watcher can emit a subdirectory path and falsely pass even if the daemon never subscribes recursively on the real filesystem. Rejection-Gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text requires actual watch coverage of subdirectories under the real watched root, which is an OS/filesystem boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp input directory on disk, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory without restart. This verifies recursive watch behavior against real filesystem events.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS indicates the core failure is local dedup decision logic (same path + time window), not inherently OS/network/subprocess. REAL-INFRA, LLM-DEP, and MULTI-STEP are all no. Rejection-Gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text is about deduplication logic itself, not watcher-to-queue handoff correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component/function directly with a controllable clock: first request for path is accepted, second within window is rejected, third after window is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names OS-emitted duplicate filesystem events; REAL-INFRA is yes by definition. MOCK-HIDE is yes because mocked single-event streams or idealized fake watchers miss duplicate-event patterns from real backends. Rejection-Gate: Integration is rejected by the phrase "operating system emits multiple filesystem events," which is concrete higher-level evidence.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename/flush), and assert exactly one processing/output for that file path.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem lifecycle condition (delete/recreate inode/path) requiring OS watcher behavior; REAL-INFRA yes. In-process fakes often preserve logical identity and hide re-subscription bugs, so MOCK-HIDE yes. Rejection-Gate: Integration is rejected because behavior text requires handling actual directory removal/recreation while running.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is cross-module ordering/state handoff (event intake -> queue/scheduler -> processor), so Unit is too low under MOCK-MISS. Integration-BRAKE: (a) no required real OS behavior is stated for correctness beyond ordering rule; (b) no AI output; (c) no multi-agentic chain; (d) no LLM stages. Rejection-Gate: System considered but rejected because behavior text does not require validating OS-specific timestamp semantics, only daemon ordering policy by creation timestamp.
behaviors.test_description: Wire watcher-event ingestion, metadata lookup abstraction, and queue/processor modules in-process; inject simultaneous arrivals with distinct creation timestamps and assert processing order follows timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real write-in-progress race at filesystem/event timing boundary; REAL-INFRA yes. MOCK-HIDE yes because fake 'complete' signals do not reproduce partial writes, flush timing, and event races seen on real OS/filesystems. Rejection-Gate: Integration is rejected since behavior explicitly depends on timing between real write progress and arrival events.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real directory, start a slow/chunked write to a file, trigger arrival, verify no processing occurs mid-write, then complete/close file and assert processing happens afterward exactly once.
